skill.name: web-search
skill.description: Run web search, answer, and page extraction workflows through the inference.sh belt CLI using Tavily and Exa apps.
skill.summary: Search the web and extract page content with Tavily and Exa through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.author: infsh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static external command and network findings are true positives because the skill documents belt CLI commands that call inference.sh, Tavily, and Exa services. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, credential theft, or executable code beyond documented CLI usage. Static weak cryptography findings are false positives from Markdown text and table syntax.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution for Search Workflows
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill documents belt CLI commands for search, extraction, answer, and related skill installation workflows. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it gives the assistant an external command path and depends on the installed CLI behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Markdown examples directly invoke belt commands. The commands are consistent with the skill purpose, and I found no evidence of arbitrary shell interpolation or malicious payloads.

medium_findings.title: Network Disclosure to Search and Extraction Providers
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: Search queries and target URLs are sent to inference.sh-backed Tavily and Exa apps. This is core functionality, but users should avoid submitting secrets, private URLs, or regulated data unless their provider agreement permits it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly describes web search and URL extraction through external services. The network behavior is intentional, but it creates clear data disclosure considerations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static weak cryptography detections do not correspond to cryptographic code or hash algorithm usage. The referenced lines contain the skill description, Markdown table headers, and a documentation link.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic implementation. The flagged locations are Markdown metadata, table text, or ordinary URLs.

low_findings.title: Documentation and Example URLs Are Expected
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Several hardcoded URLs are documentation links, an image asset, or example.com placeholders. They do not by themselves indicate hidden callbacks or credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in documentation and match the stated search and extraction purpose. No evidence found of covert endpoints, encoded destinations, or automatic background requests.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Examples Invoke a Networked CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill examples run belt app commands that can contact external services. This pattern should remain constrained to the documented belt command surface and should not pass untrusted shell fragments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and legitimate, but any assistant-operated shell command can become risky if later modified to include unsafe user-controlled shell text.

dangerous_patterns.title: URL Extraction Sends Page Targets Off Host
dangerous_patterns.description: The extraction examples submit URLs to Tavily or Exa apps. This can reveal private page locations or internal hostnames if users provide sensitive URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The extraction examples clearly send URL values to remote apps. The risk depends on user-provided targets, but the disclosure path is concrete.

content.value_statement: Research tasks often require current sources, clean page text, and quick factual answers. This skill provides Tavily and Exa workflows through the inference.sh belt CLI.
actual_capabilities: Runs Tavily search assistant queries through the belt CLI.
Extracts clean content from one or more provided web URLs.
Runs Exa search for relevant links with contextual snippets.
Requests direct factual answers through Exa answer workflows.
Combines search or extracted content with Claude model prompts.
Lists related inference.sh skills for broader tool coverage.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and a logged-in account.
Search quality depends on Tavily, Exa, and provider availability.
It does not independently verify every returned source.
Submitted queries and URLs may be processed by external services.

use_cases.title: Current Topic Research
use_cases.description: Gather recent web results, source links, and short answers before writing a brief or report.

use_cases.title: RAG Source Collection
use_cases.description: Find and extract relevant pages for retrieval-augmented generation pipelines.

use_cases.title: Claim Verification
use_cases.description: Search for supporting sources and compare extracted page content against a claim.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Web Search
prompt_templates.scenario: Find starter sources on a topic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the web for reliable sources about [topic]. Return the best sources, short summaries, and publication dates when available.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Article Content
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn one or more pages into clean text.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the main content from these URLs: [URLs]. Remove navigation, ads, and unrelated page text.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Search Providers
prompt_templates.scenario: Use both Tavily and Exa for broader coverage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for [topic] with Tavily and Exa. Compare overlap, unique results, and source quality.

prompt_templates.title: Research With Synthesis
prompt_templates.scenario: Build a source-backed answer from search and extraction.
prompt_templates.prompt: Research [question], extract the most relevant sources, then synthesize the answer with citations and unresolved uncertainties.

output_examples.input: Search for recent guidance on building AI research agents.
output_examples.output: A ranked source list with short summaries, relevance notes, and links to provider results.

output_examples.input: Extract the main content from two public article URLs.
output_examples.output: Clean article text, detected images when available, and a concise note about extraction completeness.

output_examples.input: Answer a factual question using Exa answer.
output_examples.output: A direct answer with supporting source references and a note when evidence is limited.

best_practices: Use precise queries with dates, entities, and source requirements.
Review returned sources before using them in important decisions.
Avoid sending private URLs, secrets, or regulated data to external providers.
anti_patterns: Do not treat search output as verified truth without source review.
Do not paste API keys, credentials, or private documents into queries.
Do not use broad vague searches when a targeted query would reduce noise.

faq.question: Does this skill require an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires the inference.sh belt CLI and uses Tavily or Exa apps for search and extraction.

faq.question: Can it search the live web from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: It provides command examples that let those tools run web search through belt when the environment allows it.

faq.question: Does it store my search queries?
faq.answer: The skill file does not define storage. Queries are sent to external providers, so review their data policies.

faq.question: Can it extract content from any URL?
faq.answer: It can request extraction from provided URLs, but access, robots rules, paywalls, and page structure may limit results.

faq.question: Is this safe for confidential research?
faq.answer: Use caution. Queries and URLs may leave your host, so avoid confidential data unless approved by policy.

faq.question: Does it replace human fact checking?
faq.answer: No. It speeds up source discovery and extraction, but users should verify important claims independently.
